pageTitle.value: Science - Perpetuating knowledge - Rolex.org

pageCaption.value: The science of precision has inspired the creation of Rolex chronometers for five generations – precision that is founded in a scientific understanding of time and materials, their origins and interaction with one another.

WaPageTitle.value: Science - Perpetuating knowledge - Rolex.org

metadescription.value: For generations, Rolex has created precision watches for expeditions and sponsored the work of scientists believing that human ingenuity can overcome any challenge.

contentTitle.value: TIME FOR THE EXACTING WORLD OF SCIENCE

contentText.value:
The science of precision has inspired the creation of Rolex chronometers for five generations – precision that is founded in a scientific understanding of time and materials, their origins and interaction with one another.

Born of an age of science, Rolex employs the ever-advancing frontier of human knowledge in the creation of its instruments – but we also enable science itself, bringing the dimension of measured time to the exploration and discovery of new worlds. Rolex supports individuals and organizations working across the sciences, believing that no challenge is so great that human wisdom, ingenuity, determination and skill cannot overcome it.

description.value: From researching the secrets of the universe, to developing technology that helps paralysed people to walk again – and numerous innovative projects in between – Rolex supports exceptional individuals and scientific organizations working at the frontier of human knowledge and improving lives. Through its Perpetual Planet Initiative, the company is helping to find solutions for our social and environmental challenges, giving hope for the future.

pageTitle.value: Grégoire Courtine's Life in motion - Perpetual Planet

pageCaption.value: Neuroscientist and Rolex Awards Laureate Grégoire Courtine has dedicated his life to the treatment of spinal cord injuries. With the help of his latest “test pilot”, his revolutionary technology is achieving results that could change the lives of millions.

WaPageTitle.value: Grégoire Courtine's Life in motion - Perpetual Planet

metadescription.value: Neuroscientist Grégoire Courtine's revolutionary "digital bridge" technology could change the lives of those with spinal cord injuries. Learn more on rolex.org.

pageTitle.value: Felix Brooks-Church's Sanku - Perpetual Planet

pageCaption.value: Social entrepreneur Felix Brooks-church’s mission is to eliminate malnutrition. His “dosifier”, a machine that fortifies flour with essential nutrients at minimal cost, reaches more than 25 million people across East Africa.

WaPageTitle.value: Sanku de Felix Brooks-Church - Perpetual Planet

metadescription.value: Felix Brooks-Church’s mission is to eliminate malnutrition across East Africa by fortifying flour with essential nutrients. Learn more on rolex.org.

pageTitle.value: Miranda Wang's Novoloop - Perpetual Planet

pageCaption.value: Miranda Wang is realizing a decade of research and innovation. With support from Rolex, she has successfully built a first-of-its-kind demonstration plant that can turn low-value plastics into high-quality materials, a major milestone towards a plastic circular economy.

WaPageTitle.value: Miranda Wang's Novoloop - Perpetual Planet

metadescription.value: Miranda Wang's company Novoloop is tackling the plastic waste crisis by putting an innovative recycling method into action. Learn more on rolex.org.

pageCaption.value: Kenyan entrepreneur Beth Koigi is deploying a piece of technology that is addressing one of the world’s most pressing and complex problems: water scarcity.

metadescription.value: Entrepreneur Beth Koigi is revolutionizing the way clean water is supplied to communities in need through her start-up, Majik Water. More on rolex.org.

pageTitle.value: Rolex Awards - Securing Nigeria's food supply

pageCaption.value: In 2010, Nigerian social entrepreneur Nnaemeka Ikegwuonu won a Rolex Award for Enterprise for creating a radio station to advise smallholder farmers. Now, his latest project, a network of solar-powered, walk-in cold rooms, is revolutionizing Nigeria’s food supply chain.

WaPageTitle.value: Rolex Awards - Securing Nigeria's food supply

metadescription.value: Social entrepreneur Nnaemeka Ikegwuonu's ColdHubs are revolutionizing Nigeria’s food supply chain by reducing post-harvest food loss. More on rolex.org.

pageCaption.value: Neuroscientist Grégoire Courtine is developing groundbreaking bioengineering technologies to treat spinal cord injury.

metadescription.value: Neurologist Grégoire Courtine is developing groundbreaking bioengineering technologies to restore mobility to paralysed people. More on rolex.org.

pageTitle.value: 2021 Rolex Awards for Enterprise Laureate: Felix Brooks-church

pageCaption.value: Poor nutrition contributes to 15,000 preventable child deaths daily worldwide. American social entrepreneur Felix Brooks-church has an answer: an ingenious system to ensure that each meal consumed by every mother and infant living in an underprivileged society contains essential, life-saving nutrients.

WaPageTitle.value: 2021 Rolex Awards for Enterprise Laureate: Felix Brooks-church

metadescription.value: Felix Brooks-church and his innovative way to tackle malnutrition is bringing new life and hope to children in Tanzania. Discover more on rolex.org.

pageTitle.value: Piyush Tewari - Saving lives on India's roads - Rolex.org

pageCaption.value: Following a distress call informing him that his cousin had died in a road accident where no bystanders came forward to support the victim, Piyush Tewari was propelled into finding solutions to this problem in India and has since dedicated himself to saving thousands of lives across the country and beyond.

WaPageTitle.value: Piyush Tewari - Saving lives on India's roads - Rolex.org

metadescription.value: Rolex Awards Laureate Piyush Tewari has drastically reduced the number of road victims in India with first aid training programs. More on rolex.org.

pageTitle.value: Rolex Awards - Eye test brings equality

pageCaption.value: A billion people across the globe suffer from readily treatable eyesight problems. At least a third of them live in places where modern optical treatment never reaches, but that is rapidly changing, thanks to the inspired vision of British ophthalmologist Andrew Bastawrous.

WaPageTitle.value: Rolex Awards - Eye test brings equality

metadescription.value: Andrew Bastawrous has deployed an easy-to-use mobile phone-based technology to test the eyesight of people across Africa and Asia. More on rolex.org.

pageTitle.value: Rolex Awards - Taking a volcano’s pulse

pageCaption.value: Eight hundred million people live in the shadow of fiery death, within striking range of one of Earth’s 500 historically active volcanoes. For Scottish volcanologist Andrew McGonigle, providing timely warning of an impending eruption is a goal that is both humanitarian and scientific.

WaPageTitle.value: Taking a volcano’s pulse - Rolex Awards

metadescription.value: Using drone technology to predict volcanic eruptions, Andrew McGonigle’s work is both humanitarian and scientific. Learn more on rolex.org.

$AppRoute:8D53B4D661FE4302B3C2D2EBC13AA9.value: Rolex Awards Laureate Miranda Wang invented a way to break down formerly unrecyclable plastics into reusable elements. Now, she has shown it can work on an industrial scale.

$AppRoute:BDB67B2ABCF74746AF5A3818C93622.value: Andrew Bastawrous’ Peek Vision has screened 10 million people for sight issues across 12 countries since its founding. Watch the video on rolex.org.

$AppRoute:B3452E90F52F575C94BACE2AF658D9.value: The science of precision has inspired the creation of Rolex chronometers for five generations – precision that is founded in a scientific understanding of time and materials, their origins and interaction with one another.

$AppRoute:893F1C4533D54FB5ABFBBFD633102F.value: Growing up in Limuru, Kenya, entrepreneur Beth Koigi was surrounded by the lush greenery of wetland swamps and rain-blessed hills.
2023 Rolex Awards Laureate: Beth Koigi - Episode 1

$AppRoute:0270A895088E45E290C0266E2F791E.value: Earth’s atmosphere contains six times as much water as all the planet’s rivers combined.
2023 Rolex Awards Laureate: Beth Koigi - Episode 2

$AppRoute:A2CFDE09C60B458B97EBDC2B4DEFD5.value: As a 2023 Rolex Awards Laureate, Beth Koigi is scaling up her project delivering solar-powered air-to-water technology to off-grid communities.
2023 Rolex Awards Laureate: Beth Koigi - Episode 3

$AppRoute:5026767E8D5B502AA1AA9BD50A071F.value: Through her environmental education park, young visionary Maritza Morales Casanova hopes to inspire a generation of young people to care for the Yucatán’s fragile environment.
Helping children to build a new world - Video

$AppRoute:573F59D029305D45AAEDA7C12C61B3.value: Englishman Les Stocker won a Rolex Award in 1990 to establish Europe’s first wildlife teaching hospital. The hospital was soon set up and continues to expand, but along the way Stocker has achieved much more, becoming a highly respected expert on first aid and rehabilitation for sick and injured wild animals.
The call of the wild - Video

$AppRoute:9864637FDC8C52528B021B1F74CD71.value: Forrest Mims III calls himself a "citizen scientist" – he has a degree in government, but no formal scientific qualifications. Yet he possesses a rare breadth of scientific and technical expertise in an age of narrow professionalism. One of his greatest achievements has been to set up a network to monitor ultraviolet radiation and ozone levels, using a hand-held device he invented himself..

$AppRoute:913FE00B11325EFEAFFEE011D09A3B.value: For four decades, the Rolex Awards for Enterprise have supported individuals with original ideas who are determined to make the world a better place. Over 100 men and women have been singled out for their spirit of enterprise.

$AppRoute:D1481C63F29A46108EF2CBBD97E4AB.value: In 2010, Nnaemeka Ikegwuonu, a Nigerian social entrepreneur, won a Rolex Award for Enterprise for creating a radio station to advise smallholder farmers.

$AppRoute:BD7E234CA1B44B3DB9344FB7A0628F.value: Around 20 million people worldwide have a spinal cord injury, which has a major impact on their quality of life.
The future of paralysis treatment - Video

$AppRoute:C88CF058E2084E65B207ADDD9817AC.value: Poor nutrition contributes to 15,000 preventable child deaths daily worldwide. American social entrepreneur Felix Brooks-church has an answer: an ingenious system to ensure that each meal consumed by every mother and infant living in an underprivileged society contains essential, life-saving nutrients. His project is bringing new life and hope to children in Tanzania, as a model for the world.
Eradicate malnutrition in Tanzania, one fortified bag at a time - Video

$AppRoute:52CBBC3D8D6F501BA64A6AFCCAD5F1.value: The Rolex Awards identify and invest in exceptional people around the world who are carrying out pioneering projects. Find out more about the Awards now.
2019 Frequently Asked Questions - The Rolex Awards

$AppRoute:2E0C0C7E35CC541CB0C5CEA15BDA01.value: In many cities worldwide, rain is regarded as a nuisance to be quickly drained away. But not in Tokyo, where, thanks to a retired civil servant, people see rainwater as a precious commodity to be captured and used for daily life.
A gift from the sky - Rolex Awards

$AppRoute:390A0482784A5FC0BC2839A2DEDD95.value: Sri Lankan surgeon Wijaya Godakumbura battled apathy and ignorance to prevent people being disfigured by burns caused by unstable, home-made lamps. His invention, the Safe Bottle Lamp, has saved many lives.
Safe lamps save lives - Rolex Awards

$AppRoute:B29BE70DD5E35562AEB59145634D60.value: Biomedical engineer Mark Kendall is revolutionizing the way life-saving vaccines are delivered, lowering the cost of immunization in the developing world and dispensing with the needle and syringe.

$AppRoute:9FAAB7F475905A89A5DE90FA0C9406.value: French engineer-turned-sailor Jacques Luc Autran made a series of journeys to bring medical relief, technical assistance and food to communities cut off by the sea.

$AppRoute:FC7E5C8574A45EDFB972466192A4E2.value: Pierre Morvan, who gave up driving taxis to become a world-renowned entomologist, has demonstrated how the study of ground beetles can improve our understanding of evolutionary change and how species are formed.
From taxis to beetles - Rolex Awards

$AppRoute:41737F7478615D7B9D356514274931.value: India has the highest number of road fatalities in the world, but thanks to Piyush Tewari, accident victims now have a much greater chance of receiving care at the scene – and surviving.

$AppRoute:A7AB04BAC6065702ACFECEE7C4D4FE.value: In remote Ladakh, a Tibetan herbal medicine has been prevented from dying out, thanks in part to the intervention of French ethno-pharmacologist Laurent Pordié.
Vital role for ancient medicine - Rolex Awards

$AppRoute:A6D9E1217217569F87B2D638EB2395.value: Appalled by the deaths of thousands of children from preventable diseases in her country, an Ethiopian schoolteacher invented an entertaining way to inform youngsters about hygiene.
The hand puppet that saves lives

$AppRoute:66EF543A1AAE5960A1BB2186649215.value: Captive breeding is the last hope for some endangered birds. American Billy Lee Lasley was working as a research endocrinologist at the San Diego Zoo when he developed a non-invasive method for determining the sex of birds.

$AppRoute:49468E78327657E388ACBFA836D6A5.value: French glaciologist Bernard Francou extracted an ice core from deep inside an Andean glacier to provide an archive of climatic change over thousands of years.
Getting to the core - Rolex Awards

$AppRoute:D011E79F4DED55EABF7DCB42D004CC.value: Multi-drug resistant bacteria are threatening the gains of modern medicine. Hosam Zowawi is fighting back with rapid superbug tests and a communications plan for the Gulf states.

$AppRoute:937EA6CA8CED5D73868A2B1FAE046A.value: Saving the lives of mothers and babies who lack emergency obstetric care is the life’s calling of Aggrey Otieno, who is empowering impoverished Nairobi slum-dwellers through a telemedicine centre.

$AppRoute:D80954F139DD5205B3C2D48C251628.value: For almost four decades, physician Aldo Lo Curto has divided the year between his medical practice in northern Italy and making trips to heal, teach and live among the world’s indigenous people.
Doctor to the world - Rolex Awards

$AppRoute:2FBA9373DCC4509D83C88FC2B9CB05.value: Prehistoric animal tracks at Pehuen Có in Argentina are threatened by rising sea levels and human destruction but the pioneering efforts of palaeontologist Teresa Manera de Bianco are helping to preserve them.
Footprints to the past - Rolex Awards

$AppRoute:DE1B7FA3D1D95D509B063987058A01.value: A young Italian woman has set up a storytelling school to preserve Afghanistan’s oral heritage, give hope to the country’s youth and spread crucial development information.

$AppRoute:C3C22333C7FF53CBABE025BD3682C8.value: Children in the wilds of Siberia no longer have to be separated from their parents to get an education thanks to Alexandra Lavrillier, who has established a mobile school that brings together a modern curriculum and the vanishing Evenk culture.
School on the taiga - Rolex Awards

$AppRoute:F626EC3E8F54549ABAD2BA5123D791.value: American scientist Steven Garrett has developed sound-powered refrigeration equipment with the potential to eliminate chemicals that damage the ozone layer.

$AppRoute:06585620981559E991CF15AC9C50E5.value: Millions of blind people in India have been left out of the smartphone revolution but designer Sumit Dagar is aiming to open the door to technology for them.

$AppRoute:9DFF10E2137A51E99FBA0593B3DF2D.value: In a few hours, overnight frost can destroy a crop of fruit. But a machine invented in Uruguay is providing a solution in orchards on several continents.
Fruit trees come in from the cold - Rolex Awards

$AppRoute:00A05935A3265F0F93AB907204B70B.value: Nancy Jones Abeiderrahmane opened Africa’s first camel milk dairy in Mauritania, bringing a paradigm shift for livestock husbandry and improving the quality of life for people living in her adoptive country.

$AppRoute:79C740593D95585DB18ECE15A4B2D3.value: In a semi-arid land with no refrigeration, food perishes quickly, causing major problems for local people. But Mohammed Bah Abba, who came from a family of potters, had a spectacularly simple solution.
Cool food in the desert - Rolex Awards

$AppRoute:9FDCCA24829B5EAC857F58C15A8D3B.value: Louis Liebenberg designed the CyberTracker to enable Kalahari Bushmen to record their observations of animals, but the technology has proved a highly versatile scientific tool.
Taking a new track - Rolex Awards

$AppRoute:20DFBA0B08725BAE8F4DD54BFD2614.value: Seventy million people worldwide use sign languages. They are divided by 126 different languages, each with its own vocabulary. However, there are very few dictionaries to bridge the gaps between these languages. A Japanese entrepreneur is creating an online sign-language dictionary to help deaf communities across the world converse.
Connecting the world with signs - Rolex Awards

$AppRoute:629513AF602159CA9A9888C7A21114.value: By combining old-fashioned altruism and 21st-century technology, a young trailblazer from California has given the world a new model for philanthropy.
March of the micro-volunteers - Rolex Awards

$AppRoute:DF58DD0CFC3F593F9DAAFFAEC758D9.value: Recognizing the magic and the science that exploring the heavens brings to school children, Gilbert Clark’s virtual observatory links hundreds of schools to the world’s most powerful telescopes.
Stars in their eyes - Rolex Awards

$AppRoute:BA18429B48D953F6A31D867B0DB230.value: By recycling discarded bicycles, David Schweidenback is giving people in the developing world the means to pursue employment, commerce and education.
Bicycle ride to a new life - Rolex Awards

$AppRoute:401E6FF59F9B50A79FE69942D9D183.value: In 1997 when Dave Irvine-Halliday saw children in Nepal trying to read in near-dark classrooms he resolved to light up remote villages using low-cost renewable energy.
Light for the world - Rolex Awards

$AppRoute:7838613F6A565506BC9DEDB27BCF3A.value: By marrying textile science with robotics, Conor Walsh, an Irish biomedical engineer, and a team of experts at the Harvard Biodesign Lab are revolutionizing how patients worldwide recover from traumas such as stroke and learn to walk again.

$AppRoute:7CFFE8907CB952769153645374A210.value: The Q Drum invented by South African architect Hans Hendrikse has improved the lives of many thousands in developing countries, providing a simple, easy means to transport up to 50 litres of water at a time.
Water drum eases burden - Rolex Awards

$AppRoute:39B468CC1F66520FA262FEE6B7B761.value: Locust swarms are capable of devouring vast fields of crops within hours. Geographer Frithjof Voss used satellite image mapping to detect locusts before they could wreak their havoc.
Halting the locust plague - Rolex Awards

$AppRoute:E5B72BC51EE05A60B8619B876080F4.value: IT specialist Arthur Zang has used his technological know-how to invent and manufacture Africa’s first computer tablet to diagnose people with heart disease.
A new kind of heart tablet - Rolex Awards

$AppRoute:63DCB1D0C77E51BDA74FFADB1D9E03.value: Millions of people live in the shadow of active volcanoes but Scottish physicist Andrew McGonigle is using drones to develop ways to predict eruptions.

$AppRoute:C1637A9D682A5EB38B2B0F94F2731C.value: Huge piles of discarded rice husks clutter many farms in Asia. Inventor Alexis Belonio has found a way to put them to practical use, at the same time as reducing pollution and saving farmers’ money in the process.
Recycling rice husks into fuel - Rolex Awards

$AppRoute:E31928B78BAF5F0E8166E7D29652FF.value: Through her environmental theme park, the visionary Mexican environmentalist Maritza Morales Casanova hopes to teach a generation of young people to care for the Yucatán’s fragile environment.
Helping children to build a new world - Rolex Awards

$AppRoute:DB9F5B14F2FD5BCFB4939AFBE9D81E.value: After rescuing his first injured hedgehog in the 1970s, English accountant Les Stocker, transformed his veterinary hobby into a world famous wildlife teaching hospital.
The call of the wild - Rolex Awards

$AppRoute:BC48780399BF46D4A363C5F91CA1C9.value: The Rolex Awards identify and invest in exceptional people around the world who are carrying out pioneering projects. Find out more about the Awards now.
The Rolex Awards - The next chapter

$AppRoute:C5324A1BEE8E5488BB22984E522BFD.value: Piyush Tewari is determined to save thousands of lives by ensuring that most accident victims in Delhi receive rapid medical care. His foundation, in partnership with hospitals and a medical science institute, has already provided training sessions in basic life support to over 2,000 police officers, as well as 500 ordinary citizens.

$AppRoute:82B53126760048C8AC23C454186666.value: Biomedical engineer and 2012 Rolex Laureate Mark Kendall is on a mission to revolutionize modern medicine.
Medical alerts from a sticker on your hand - Video

$AppRoute:10A7B1F08E085EE98DBF506591DDAE.value: Scottish physicist Andrew McGonigle is developing a reliable way to predict eruptions, using an unmanned, small-scale helicopter to measure gases that escape from volcanic vents. His combination of science and advanced technology has the potential to save thousands of lives.

$AppRoute:49407E314D77590297CB9939E95160.value: Andrew Bastawrous is an ophthalmologist from England whose dream is to treat the millions of visually impaired people living in low-income countries. He has begun a project in remote areas of Kenya, where there is little eye care but good mobile phone technology. His solution is to use smartphones as a portable eye examination kit, and eventually make this technology available around the world.

$AppRoute:9BFB69B6BE424550BEA2F8784AC9F6.value: Motivated by the unnecessary death of his cousin in a road accident where bystanders typically ignored the victim’s plight, successful Indian businessman and 2010 Rolex Awards for Enterprise Laureate Piyush Tewari gave up his career to concentrate on helping to save the lives of his compatriots on the country’s chaotic roads.
Saving lives on India’s roads - Video

$AppRoute:ACE0B978BBC354F798E3DF119C9B35.value: In south-eastern Siberia, a nomadic people are trying to preserve their way of life against the march of modern society. For eight years Alexandra Lavrillier, a brilliant French ethnologist, has been fighting alongside them to save their heritage, setting up a nomadic school that will give Evenk children the chance to receive a modern education without having to sacrifice their ancestral traditions.

$AppRoute:988393C6DB4B51539607A8FF1AA7CB.value: Italian social entrepreneur Selene Biffi has set up a storytelling school in Kabul that will preserve Afghanistan’s rich oral heritage and spread crucial development information.

$AppRoute:2263A9CC9A56547EB611AE8F51C1D4.value: Argentinian palaeontologist and geologist Teresa Manera de Bianco is struggling to save a unique collection of animal footprints made 12,000 years ago. The three-kilometre-long site is now part of the Atlantic coastline near Teresa’s home, but 12,000 years ago it was an inland pond teeming with birds and mammals.

$AppRoute:1767B388FF315FFFB8022F7F8D8E6F.value: Professor Mark Kendall is working on the development of the needle-free ‘Nanopatch’, to revolutionize delivery of vaccines in the developing world making them cheaper and potentially saving the lives of the millions of people who die from infectious diseases.

$AppRoute:7D2D46C0C842574F8FC7E7CC1D2613.value: Pierre Morvan, a self-taught expert on insects, has made his mark in science by exploring insect populations in a region often out of bounds to professional scientists – the isolated and inaccessible regions of the Himalayas..

$AppRoute:6DC7EE7877FE5EFBAF0F49646E2C68.value: For more than a decade, a resolute surgeon in Sri Lanka has battled apathy and ignorance to save people from disfigurement and death by fire caused by home-made lamps. Wijaya Godakumbura, who won a Rolex Award in 1998, has a simple solution to this devastating problem — the Safe Bottle Lamp.

$AppRoute:029D1BA6E49559C18DD885BBE54A2E.value: Women doctors for telemedicine in Pakistan
Women doctors for telemedicine in Pakistan - Video

$AppRoute:765389DA817C581F834D193F586302.value: Superbugs, resistant to antibiotics, are a threat to humanity. Saudi Arabian microbiologist Hosam Zowawi is combating the misuse of antibiotics by developing groundbreaking diagnostic tests and an awareness campaign in the Gulf states.

$AppRoute:3E653250AC275A68AF8207D586B8D1.value: After years of preparation, engineer-turned-sailor Jacques Luc Autran set sail in 1987 on the Listaos, a rebuilt trawler, for the Indian Ocean and the isolated archipelagos that dot this area. Supported by the association Seamen without Borders, which he created, Jacques embarked on a series of journeys that would fulfil a long-held dream: to bring medical relief, technical assistance and food to communities cut off from civilization by the sea.

$AppRoute:C2B0B0B91FC153979613EC7E5C93EA.value: Help paralysed people walk again - Video

$AppRoute:512FCE25F1A15EC28900D567B9D426.value: Tibetan medicine has been practised for more than 1,000 years in Ladakh, in India’s far north. The very survival of this complex system of healing, ritual and belief — known as Amchi medicine — was threatened by the 20th century’s huge social changes. But over the past six years French anthropologist and ethno-pharmacologist Laurent Pordié has led a campaign that will help ensure Ladakhis continue to benefit from Amchi medicine for generations to come.
Vital role for ancient medicine - Video

$AppRoute:D7A31CDA6F5259329E25128B4F4E57.value: Rapid malaria testing with no blood sample
Rapid malaria testing with no blood sample - Video

$AppRoute:A9A1EFE19F5153799476009CE7357C.value: Bruktawit Tigabu will produce new episodes of Tsehai Loves Learning to teach preschool children and their parents basic health education. The programme uses songs, stories and simple graphics to make health concepts easy to grasp.
The hand puppet that saves lives - Video

$AppRoute:D2EE53EA7FC051598F789743EBBBE2.value: Aggrey Otieno will build a telemedicine centre with a 24-hour, on-call doctor and van in a Nairobi slum, thereby helping to save the lives of mothers and babies who lack access to emergency obstetric care.
A mobile lifeline for mothers - Video

$AppRoute:D6C24FFB5F3E558997C52A50FB1841.value: Physician Aldo Lo Curto has worked as a doctor in almost 40 countries in the past 20 years, putting himself at the service of humanity. This "volunteer travelling doctor" and 1993 Rolex Laureate spends half the year in his medical practice in Canzo, in northern Italy, and the rest of his time healing, teaching and living among indigenous people on several continents.

$AppRoute:E28E3CDB2588596DAF273BC958EBE5.value: Technologist Topher White is giving scientists and conservationists a unique view of wildlife in the world’s rainforests – through an alert system he originally developed to detect illegal logging using old mobile phones.
Electronic ears to listen to the rainforests - Video

$AppRoute:EFB01872DC105DD49376933A7E9917.value: Steven Garrett, a 1993 Rolex Award Laureate, has been developing sound-powered refrigeration equipment for over a decade with the ultimate hope that this environmentally friendly technology will find its way from his laboratory to everyone’s homes.

$AppRoute:DDB31AA64B695037B3829B4C5C6147.value: Sumit Dagar is developing a Braille smartphone that will help India’s millions of blind people access the digital world.

$AppRoute:AC51BBFD04655CAE9F42A7BEDB5CEC.value: Oscar Ekponimo, of Nigeria, is solving an old problem –  malnutrition caused by poverty – with a 21st century invention. His cloud-based app, Chowberry, alerts retailers when products reach the end of shelf life. Discounted food can then be offered to relief agencies, cutting waste. Ekponimo, who suffered from hunger as a child, developed his app to help the millions of Nigerians who do not get enough to eat.
Software that cuts food waste - Video

$AppRoute:D9B889751B8950979FA5AA0619EA42.value: When Nancy Abeiderrahmane opened Africa’s first camel milk dairy in Nouakchott in 1989, most Mauritanians wouldn’t think of buying — let alone drinking — processed milk from local livestock. Today, their attitudes are completely changed. Nancy, whose tenacity, drive and acumen are rarely matched, is behind this amazing transformation, and her work has made an important contribution to improving the quality of life in Mauritania.

$AppRoute:B1E939C583B95FFF9F79F0E00E1765.value: Nigerian teacher Mohammed Bah Abba was motivated by his concern for the rural poor and by his interest in indigenous African technology to seek a practical, local solution to these problems. His extremely simple and inexpensive earthenware "pot-in-pot" cooling device, based on a principle of physics already known in ancient Egypt, has revolutionized lives in this semi-desert area.
Cool food in the desert - Video

$AppRoute:2FC6EF5AFB2C5D25A0434FA68FC904.value: If 25 year-old Canadian entrepreneur Miranda Wang fulfils her goal, a third of the world’s plastic waste – which now chokes landfills, rivers and oceans – could be converted into new wealth.

$AppRoute:41F0A68F7E345164A9F45F94E3F328.value: The world’s 70 million hearing-impaired people use 126 different languages, each with its own grammar and vocabulary. However, there are very few dictionaries to bridge the gaps between these languages. A Japanese entrepreneur, Junto Ohki, has created SLinto, an online sign-language dictionary that crowdsources signs –which are input with a special keyboard – to help deaf communities across the world converse.
Connecting the world with signs - Video

$AppRoute:E02A578AFAF55C9087EB026D3D14DC.value: CyberTracker, the brainchild of 1998 Laureate Louis Liebenberg, is a handheld device originally developed to modernize the ancient skill of tracking. While it has proven highly successful for its original purpose, Louis has discovered that its software has revolutionary potential to reduce some of the negative impacts of climate change; CyberTracker technology can monitor, predict and help prevent irreversible damage to our ecosystems.

$AppRoute:D2DA5A058C275E5A9DAAC0DA14D6B5.value: Tapping into the latest trends in information and telecommunications technology, Jacob Colker has combined volunteering, the internet and mobile phones to pioneer a new form of activism in which almost anyone with a smartphone can devote spare minutes — waiting for the bus or to see the doctor — to a useful charitable or scientific task.

$AppRoute:3A63AC5803F55127BE66762E0E3179.value: By giving new life to used bicycles, David Schweidenback, who won a Rolex Award in 2000, is improving the lives of people in many developing countries. Pedals for Progress, the organisation he founded in 1991, has collected and exported more than 159,000 bikes – and has extended its activities to sewing-machines and new spare parts.
Bicycle ride to a new life? - Video

$AppRoute:6943E866D90F59109B84B605BD9B01.value: An Irish biomedical engineer based at Harvard, Conor Walsh is addressing the acute challenges faced by stroke victims as they learn to walk again. He is developing a soft robotic suit that can be worn under clothes and that trains muscles, limbs and joints to function again. This exciting new technology should revolutionize treatment for the millions of people who suffer from physical disabilities.

$AppRoute:1F09EDC2CB6D587CB63520B97CD7A5.value: Electrical engineer Dave Irvine-Halliday realised that a single 0.1 watt, white-light emitting diode supplies enough light for a child to read by. The simple but revolutionary technology supplied to homes by his Light Up The World Foundation can light an entire rural village with less energy than that used by a single, conventional, 100 watt light bulb.

$AppRoute:3CE952F3CDDB5CBA9E248903ABF89C.value: Arthur Zang is revolutionizing cardiac care in Cameroon, where heart disease is rising. His invention – a tablet-computer heart monitor called a Cardio Pad –  needs only mobile phone network coverage to diagnose heart disease anywhere, any time.
A new kind of heart tablet - Video

$AppRoute:522085B25A5F41ECA83CF819A00C26.value: Ophthalmologist Andrew Bastawrous is pursuing a vision to bring clear eyesight to millions of people around the world. Discover the video on rolex.org.

$AppRoute:E54B86E7F8C8499E917AEA49AAC913.value: Eight hundred million people live in the shadow of fiery death, within striking range of one of Earth’s 500 historically active volcanoes. For Scottish volcanologist Andrew McGonigle, providing timely warning of an impending eruption is a goal that is both humanitarian and scientific.

$AppRoute:CDC07E09FB385A02B6325C5260193F.value: It appears the page you tried to view is not available. Please click here to return to the homepage, or visit one of our other pages via the footer below.

$AppRoute:88A25A75233055EA94C60D25473C94.value: Billy Lee Lasley’s invention of a non-invasive method for determining the sex of birds — designed to help protect endangered species such as eagles — has slowly been adopted by researchers around the world. Perhaps more importantly, his work has proved to have other uses, including monitoring the reproductive health of women exposed to chemicals in the environment.
